steps.title: See the complex list
steps.content: The sentence lists three material categories, each followed by a "such as" example: (1) natural debris, such as dried leaves; (2) man-made trash, such as plastic bags; (3) more traditional art supplies, such as tree glue. Because each item already contains an internal comma, the items themselves must be separated by semicolons.

steps.title: Apply the rule and check what comes after the blank
steps.content: After the blank, the sentence reads "such as plastic bags; and more traditional art supplies" — that semicolon already separates item 2 from item 3, confirming the list uses semicolons. So the first separator (after "leaves") must also be a semicolon, and the comma after "trash" must precede its "such as" example.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'leaves, man-made trash:': uses a comma where a semicolon is needed between list items, and a colon improperly introduces "such as plastic bags." Violates the complex-list rule. ✗
  • Choice B — 'leaves; man-made trash,': semicolon correctly separates the first two items; comma correctly attaches "such as plastic bags" to "man-made trash." ✓
  • Choice C — 'leaves, man-made trash,': comma between "leaves" and "man-made trash" blurs the boundary between list items in a complex list. ✗
  • Choice D — 'leaves; man-made trash;': the second semicolon wrongly separates "man-made trash" from its own "such as" example; that connection needs a comma. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
